content.text: Plugins

are packaged extensions that add domain-specific capabilities to a Cowork workspace. Where connectors give a goal access to external data, plugins shape how the engine behaves across all goals in a workspace or project — adding reusable skill commands, always-on instructions, custom connectors, and reference resources as a single installable unit.

A plugin authored for a legal team might include a contract-review skill command, instructions that enforce citation formatting, and a reference file listing the firm's clause library. A plugin for a sales team might add a CRM connector, a deal-memo skill, and instructions to always output in the company voice. Both are installed with a single click and activate for every goal in the target workspace.
This topic explains what plugins are made of, how they are installed and scoped, and how to evaluate whether you need a plugin or simpler project-level configuration.
What a Plugin Contains

A plugin is a bundle of up to four component types. None are mandatory individually — a plugin can consist of just one component, such as a set of skill commands with no extra instructions or connectors.
Skills (slash commands)
Skills are the most common plugin component. A skill is a named, reusable goal template that users invoke with a slash command prefix — for example,
/legal-review
or
/deal-memo
. When a user types a skill name, Cowork pre-populates a goal with the template text and any required inputs the skill specifies.
Skill names are defined by the plugin author and must be unique within a workspace
A skill template can include placeholder fields that the user fills in before running
Skills reduce goal-authoring friction for repeated workflows — users invoke a skill rather than writing the full goal from scratch each time
Multiple skills can be bundled in one plugin; a sales plugin might include /deal-memo, /objection-brief, and /call-prep
Instructions

Plugin instructions are always-on directives that the engine includes in the context of every goal run in the workspace or project where the plugin is active. Unlike project memory (which is scoped to a project), plugin instructions are inherited everywhere the plugin applies.
Instructions typically enforce output format rules, persona, or compliance constraints
They merge with the goal's own context — they do not override the goal text
Example: "All outputs must include a confidence level for each factual claim" or "Use British English spelling"
Instructions should be kept short and unambiguous; long instruction blocks dilute their effectiveness
Connectors

A plugin can bundle a connector that is not available in the base Cowork app. This might be an internal company API, a vertical-specific SaaS tool, or an aggregated data source. Plugin connectors are configured by the plugin author and may require separate OAuth authorization by the installing user.
Plugin connectors appear alongside the built-in connectors once the plugin is installed
Authorization is separate from plugin installation — installing a plugin does not automatically grant access to its connector
Not all plugins include a connector — many are skill-only or instruction-only
Resources

Resources are static reference files included with the plugin — schemas, glossaries, clause libraries, brand guidelines, or any document the engine should be able to reference when running goals in this workspace. Resources are embedded in the plugin bundle and are accessible to the engine without a connector call.
Resources are read-only — the engine can reference them but not modify them
Suitable for documents that change infrequently (style guides, compliance checklists, term lists)
For frequently updated reference data, a Drive connector pointing to a live document is preferable
Plugin Scope: Workspace vs Project
When you install a plugin, you choose its scope: the entire workspace or a specific project. Scope determines which goals see the plugin's instructions and skills.
Scope level
Who sees the plugin?
Best for
Workspace
All users and all projects in the workspace
Company-wide standards, shared connector access, org-wide skill library
Project
Only goals within the specific project
Domain-specific workflows (e.g. a legal review plugin scoped to the Legal project)
Personal
Only your own goals
Plugins you are evaluating, personal productivity tools
Workspace-level plugins are set by workspace admins. Project-level plugins can be installed by project owners. Personal plugins are installed in your own settings and do not affect teammates.
Plugin precedence and conflicts

When multiple plugins are active — a workspace plugin and a project plugin — their instructions merge additively. Skill name collisions are resolved by specificity: a project-scoped plugin's skill name overrides a same-named workspace-scoped skill, so teams can specialise a workflow without changing the org-wide default.
Instructions from all active plugins are concatenated in the engine context — order is workspace → project → personal
If two plugins define the same instruction in contradictory ways, the more specific (project-level) instruction takes precedence
Connector name collisions are rare but resolved the same way — most specific scope wins
Where Plugins Come From
The Cowork marketplace

Anthropic and third-party developers publish plugins in the Cowork marketplace. Marketplace plugins are versioned and reviewed before listing. Installing a marketplace plugin takes one click; updates are applied automatically unless your workspace admin has configured manual update approval.
Browse by category (sales, legal, engineering, research, writing, operations)
Read the plugin's component manifest before installing — know what instructions and connectors you are activating
Popular marketplace plugins are maintained by their authors; check the last-updated date before installing older ones
Team-built plugins

Workspace admins can author plugins directly in Cowork's plugin editor and publish them to the workspace's internal library. This is the primary path for proprietary tools, internal API connectors, and company-specific skill libraries.

Example: internal engineering plugin
Skills:
  /incident-brief    — template for on-call incident summaries
  /pr-review         — structured pull request review with checklist
  /runbook-draft     — skeleton runbook from incident notes

Instructions:
  Always include severity classification (P0–P3) in incident summaries.
  Reference the on-call rotation at rota.company.internal when naming responders.

Connectors:
  PagerDuty (internal)  — read active incidents, escalation history
  GitHub (org-scoped)   — read PR details and commit history

Resources:
  severity-definitions.md   — P0–P3 criteria and SLA table
  on-call-runbook-template.md
Personal plugins

Individual users can create plugins in their personal settings that only apply to their own goals. This is a useful place to iterate on a skill template before proposing it as a workspace plugin, or to maintain personal productivity shortcuts without affecting teammates.
Installing a Plugin
Navigate to Settings → Plugins in Cowork
Choose Browse Marketplace or Create Plugin
For marketplace: select the plugin, review its component manifest, and click Install
Choose the scope: workspace (admin only), project, or personal
If the plugin includes a connector, authorize it in the subsequent OAuth step
The plugin is now active — skills appear in the slash-command picker, instructions are active on all new goals
Reviewing a plugin before installing

Before installing any plugin, review its manifest in the plugin detail panel. The manifest lists every component: each skill's template text, the full instructions text, any connector API domains the plugin will access, and any resource files bundled. You should review this before activating a plugin in a shared workspace.
Instructions you cannot modify: once installed, the instruction text runs for every goal in scope — if it conflicts with existing project memory, remove one
Connector domains: verify the plugin only calls APIs your org permits
Resources: confirm embedded files are appropriate and not outdated
Skill names: check for collisions with skills already active in the workspace
Plugin vs Project Memory vs Goal Text
Plugins, project memory, and goal-level instructions are three distinct ways to provide context to the engine. Choosing the right level avoids duplication and keeps the system predictable.
Use case
Best approach
Why
Reusable skill for many users
Plugin (skill component)
One install activates it workspace-wide; no copy-pasting goal templates
Persistent rules for a project
Project memory
Scoped to the project, visible to all members, editable by project owner
One-time instructions for a goal
Goal text
No configuration needed; lives with the goal
Enforced output format across all goals
Plugin (instruction component)
Cannot be accidentally omitted; applies without goal authors having to remember it
Reference doc that changes monthly
Drive connector + goal
Always reads the live doc; not bundled as a static resource
Private API not available in base app
Plugin (connector component)
Makes the connector available without requiring each user to configure it manually
Plugin Anatomy in Depth
To understand what activating a plugin does to the engine's context, it helps to trace through each component type in more detail — specifically how the engine sees it on every goal run.
How skills appear in the engine

When a user invokes a skill with a slash command, the engine receives the skill's template text substituted with any user-provided values, as if the user had typed the full goal manually. The skill template becomes the goal input — skills do not run in addition to the goal, they become the goal.

Example skill template (defined in plugin):
/deal-memo <account_name> <deal_stage>

Template text:
Prepare a deal memo for {{account_name}}, currently at {{deal_stage}} stage.
Read the account's most recent 30 emails from Gmail (search: from: or to: @{{account_name}}).
Extract: key decision-makers mentioned, stated objections, agreed next steps,
and any commercial terms discussed.
Write the memo to a new Google Doc in the "Active Deals" folder on Drive.
Format: Executive Summary → Stakeholders → Objections → Next Steps → Terms.
The user types
/deal-memo Acme Q3-Close
and the engine receives the full template with substitutions applied. The skill is the entire goal — the user supplies only the variable parts.
How instructions are merged

Plugin instructions are prepended to the engine's base system context. They are always present, regardless of what the goal says. Multiple active plugins each contribute their instructions, concatenated in scope order (workspace → project → personal).

Instructions work best as short, specific directives. Vague instructions like "be thorough" consume context without changing behavior. Precise instructions like "cite the source URL for every factual claim" produce measurable changes in output.
Good instruction: "Always output JSON with schema { items: [], total: number } when the goal requests a list of items"
Good instruction: "Do not include personally identifiable information in any output written to shared Drive files"
Weak instruction: "Try to be accurate and helpful" — this is already the engine's default
Overly long instructions (300+ words) crowd the context and reduce their own effectiveness
How resources are accessed

Plugin resources are embedded as text in the plugin bundle. The engine can access them when it needs reference data — it does not load all resources on every goal, but loads them on demand when the goal or a skill template references them explicitly or when the engine's reasoning determines the resource is relevant.

Referencing a resource in a skill template:
Classify the contract clause from the user's input using the clause taxonomy
defined in the "clause-library.md" resource (bundled with this plugin).
Return the taxonomy ID, primary category, and the matching canonical example.

Resources are read-only snapshots — they reflect the state of the file when the plugin was last published. For documents that change regularly, connect a Drive file instead and read it live from the goal.
Designing an Effective Plugin

A well-designed plugin is narrow, composable, and testable. The most common mistake is bundling too many skills and too many instructions into one plugin, making it hard to diagnose when something is not working.
One domain, one plugin
A "Legal" plugin covers legal review workflows — not general writing, not financial analysis
Separate plugins for separate teams makes it easy to enable/disable scopes without side effects
If two plugins' instructions conflict, isolating them is much harder after the fact
Small plugins are also easier to update — changing one skill does not risk breaking unrelated instructions
Skill template quality
A skill template is a goal — all the goal-writing principles apply. The best skill templates:
State the primary data source explicitly (connector name or specific file path)
Define the exact output format and destination (Drive doc, Slack message, inline)
Include a fallback instruction for when a data source is unavailable
Use {{placeholder}} syntax only for values that genuinely vary per invocation
Are tested against real data before publishing to the workspace
Instructions checklist
Before adding an instruction to a plugin, ask these questions:
Is this instruction enforceable? (Can the engine actually follow it, or is it aspirational?)
Does this instruction add to the default behavior, or does it just restate what the engine already does?
Will this instruction conflict with any goals that users of this plugin commonly run?
Is it under 50 words? Longer instructions should be a resource or project memory entry instead
Managing Plugins in a Workspace
Workspace admins control which plugins are active at the workspace level. Project owners control project-scoped plugins. Both use the same Plugins panel in Settings.
Viewing active plugins

The Plugins panel shows all currently active plugins, their scope level, their installed version, and the date of last update. Clicking a plugin shows its full manifest — a read-only view of the instructions, skill templates, and resource files it contributes.
Disabling a plugin

Disabling a plugin immediately removes its instructions from the engine context and hides its skill commands from the slash-command picker. Goals that are already running are not interrupted — the change takes effect on the next goal invocation.
Disabling does not delete user data — goals run with the plugin remain in history
Skills invoked before disabling remain in the goal history as plain text; they just cannot be re-invoked
If a connector is bundled in the plugin, disabling the plugin suspends the connector — existing Drive files or Slack posts made via the connector are unaffected
Updating a plugin

Marketplace plugins update automatically by default. Workspace admins can configure manual update approval if the org needs to review changes before they take effect. Team-built plugins are updated by republishing from the plugin editor — the new version takes effect immediately for all workspace members.
Event
Effect on running goals
Effect on future goals
Plugin installed
None — already running goals are unaffected
Skills appear; instructions active; connector available
Plugin disabled
None — running goals complete normally
Skills hidden; instructions removed from context
Plugin updated (auto)
None — update applies to next goal invocation
New skill templates and instructions take effect
Plugin connector re-auth needed
None — connector error on next use
Goals using the connector will fail until re-authorized
Real-World Plugin Examples
Concrete examples clarify what to bundle together and what to keep separate.
Engineering team plugin

Skills:
  /incident-brief <severity> <system>  — on-call incident summary
  /postmortem <incident_id>            — 5-whys postmortem draft
  /pr-summary <pr_url>                 — structured PR description

Instructions:
  Every incident brief must include a severity classification (P0–P3)
  and a single-sentence impact statement as the first paragraph.

Connectors:
  GitHub (org-scoped)    — read PR and commit data
  PagerDuty (internal)   — active incidents and escalation history

Resources:
  severity-matrix.md     — P0–P3 criteria, SLAs, and escalation contacts
Content team plugin

Skills:
  /blog-brief <topic> <audience>    — editorial brief with outline
  /social-set <asset_url>           — 3-platform social caption set
  /seo-audit <page_url>             — on-page SEO checklist review

Instructions:
  All content must use the active voice.
  Never begin a sentence with "Delve", "Leverage", or "Harness".
  Product names must match the canonical list in the brand-names resource.

Resources:
  brand-names.md        — approved product and feature names with spelling
  tone-of-voice.md      — brand voice adjectives and example phrases

Notice that the content plugin has no connector — all its data comes from the goals themselves (URLs, Drive files the user references). A connector is added only when the plugin's skills need consistent access to a specific external source.
Summary

Plugins are the primary extensibility mechanism in Cowork. A plugin bundles skills (slash-command templates), always-on instructions, optional connectors, and reference resources into a single installable unit. Plugins are scoped to a workspace, project, or personal level, and multiple plugins compose additively.
Skills are the core component — reusable goal templates invoked with /name commands
Instructions add always-on engine directives without requiring users to include them in every goal
Connectors in plugins extend the base set of available data sources for all workspace goals
Resources provide embedded reference files the engine can access without a connector
Install from the marketplace, build internally, or create personal plugins for individual use
Review the plugin manifest before installing in a shared workspace — instructions and connector scopes are active for all goals once installed
